request: Migrate pkgs/id web UI from HTMX→Datastar and custom CSS→DaisyUI/Tailwind. Keep WebSockets as-is. Design decisions needed for server architecture, build pipeline, theme strategy, and migration phasing.

server-arch.scope: Server response architecture: How Axum routes should return SSE responses for Datastar instead of plain HTML partials. Datastar uses text/event-stream with merge-fragments events. This fundamentally changes every route handler.

questions.text:
How should we restructure Axum route handlers for Datastar?

Datastar expects SSE (text/event-stream) responses instead of plain HTML. Currently, routes check HX-Request header to return partials vs full pages. We need a new approach.

cons: SSE adds complexity vs plain HTML responses
Debugging SSE streams is harder than HTML responses
options.description: Every route returns SSE with datastar-merge-fragments events. One shell route (GET /) serves the HTML skeleton. All navigation triggers @get() which returns SSE fragment merges. Create a DatastarResponse builder in Rust.
options.label: Full SSE Architecture (Recommended)
pros: Idiomatic Datastar - uses the framework as designed
Signals enable reactive state (search, filters) without extra wiring
Server controls exactly what DOM gets updated

cons: Two patterns to maintain (regular HTML + SSE)
Client router adds custom JS code
options.description: Use a lightweight client-side router (or History API) for page navigation (files→peers→settings). Use Datastar SSE only for dynamic operations (search, pagination, auto-refresh). Full pages served as regular HTML.
options.label: Hybrid: Client Router + Datastar for Dynamic Parts
pros: Simpler for navigation - regular HTML responses
Datastar only where it adds value (reactivity)

options.description: Use Datastar signals for state but handle navigation with View Transitions API + fetch for HTML fragments. Datastar SSE only for real-time updates (peers refresh, search-as-you-type).
options.label: Datastar with ViewTransition/Fetch API

config.question:
How should we restructure Axum route handlers for Datastar?

Datastar expects SSE (text/event-stream) responses instead of plain HTML. Currently, routes check HX-Request header to return partials vs full pages. We need a new approach.

questions.text: How should we handle the response layer abstraction for Datastar SSE?

config.question: How should we handle the response layer abstraction for Datastar SSE?
config.context: [Server response architecture: How Axum routes should return SSE responses for Datastar instead of plain HTML partials. Datastar uses text/event-stream with merge-fragments events. This fundamentally changes every route handler.] You chose full SSE for all Datastar routes. Now I need to understand the abstraction pattern. Currently routes return Html<String> or check HX-Request headers. With Datastar, every interactive route returns text/event-stream with merge-fragments events. We need a clean Rust-side API for building these responses.

options.label: SSE Response Builder (typed helper)
options.description: Create a DsResponse builder that chains .fragment(selector, html), .signal(key, value), .execute(script) calls and serializes to SSE format. Routes return DsResponse instead of Html<String>.
pros: Easy to unit test fragments independently
cons: Need to cover all Datastar event types

options.label: Template macro approach
options.description: Create a ds_response!() macro that takes template expressions inline and wraps them in SSE event format. Minimal abstraction, close to the metal.
pros: Familiar if you already use format!()/html! macros
cons: Harder to compose complex multi-fragment responses

options.label: Use Axum's built-in Sse<Stream> type
options.description: Leverage axum::response::Sse with tokio streams. Each route returns Sse(stream) where the stream yields Event objects. Supports keep-alive and multi-event sequences natively.
pros: Can send multiple events over time
cons: More boilerplate per route for simple single-shot responses
Stream setup overhead for non-streaming cases

server-arch.finding:
Server architecture decision: Full SSE conversion with an SSE builder abstraction layer.

1. **All routes return SSE (text/event-stream)**: Every Axum handler that currently returns HTML partials (checking HX-Request header) will be converted to return SSE streams using Datastar's merge-fragments event format. No hybrid HTMX/Datastar coexistence on the response side.

2. **SSE Builder pattern**: Create a reusable `SseResponse` builder (likely a struct with methods like `.merge_fragment()`, `.merge_signals()`, `.remove_fragments()`) that wraps Axum's SSE support. This abstracts away the raw event-stream formatting so route handlers stay clean and declarative. The builder handles:
   - Setting `Content-Type: text/event-stream`
   - Formatting `datastar-merge-fragments` events with proper `data: fragments ...` lines
   - Supporting merge modes (morph, inner, outer, etc.)
   - Signal updates via `datastar-merge-signals` events
   - Multiple fragments in a single SSE response stream

3. **Full-page vs partial logic**: Instead of checking `HX-Request` header, the Datastar approach uses the same SSE endpoints for both — initial page loads serve full HTML (standard GET), while Datastar-initiated requests hit SSE endpoints that return fragments. The routing distinction becomes: regular GET → full page template, `data-on-*` triggered requests → SSE fragment endpoints.

4. **WebSockets unchanged**: Existing WebSocket connections remain as-is; this decision only affects HTTP request/response handlers.

build-pipeline.scope: Build pipeline changes: Adding Tailwind CSS v4 + DaisyUI v5 to the Bun-based build system. Dependency management, CSS compilation, manifest integration.

questions.text:
How should we add Tailwind CSS v4 + DaisyUI v5 to the build pipeline?

Currently: Bun builds TS→JS with content hashing, build-css.ts concatenates CSS files, manifest.json maps hashed filenames.

cons: Need to configure content paths for class scanning
options.description: Install @tailwindcss/cli + daisyui as Bun dev deps. Replace build-css.ts with: bunx @tailwindcss/cli -i web/styles/input.css -o dist/styles.css --minify. CSS-first config: @import 'tailwindcss' + @plugin 'daisyui' + custom themes in input.css. Keep build-manifest.ts for content hashing.
options.label: Tailwind CLI via Bun (Recommended)
pros: DaisyUI v5 native CSS plugin support

options.description: Use PostCSS as the CSS processor with @tailwindcss/postcss plugin. Integrate into existing Bun build scripts. More control over the CSS pipeline.
options.label: PostCSS with @tailwindcss/postcss
pros: Can add other PostCSS plugins (autoprefixer, etc.)

options.description: Replace Bun bundling with Vite. Native Tailwind plugin, HMR in dev, optimized production builds. Bigger change but modern DX.
options.label: Migrate to Vite + @tailwindcss/vite

config.question:
How should we add Tailwind CSS v4 + DaisyUI v5 to the build pipeline?

Currently: Bun builds TS→JS with content hashing, build-css.ts concatenates CSS files, manifest.json maps hashed filenames.

questions.text: How should Tailwind CSS be processed during development?

config.question: How should Tailwind CSS be processed during development?
config.context:
[Build pipeline changes: Adding Tailwind CSS v4 + DaisyUI v5 to the Bun-based build system. Dependency management, CSS compilation, manifest integration.] You chose Tailwind CLI via Bun. Tailwind v4 uses a Rust-based standalone CLI that's very fast. The key question is how the dev workflow integrates: does Bun orchestrate Tailwind as a child process, or do you run them separately?

I'm recommending the single Bun script approach because it keeps the existing pattern of `bun run build` doing everything, and Tailwind v4's CLI is fast enough that a watch mode child process adds minimal complexity.

options.label: Bun script orchestrates Tailwind CLI
options.description: build-css.ts spawns `tailwindcss` CLI as a subprocess. Single `bun run dev` command handles both JS bundling and CSS. Manifest generation stays unified.

options.label: Parallel npm scripts (concurrently)
options.description: package.json `dev` script runs Bun watch + Tailwind watch in parallel via `concurrently` or `&`. Simpler per-process but two output streams.

options.label: Tailwind as PostCSS plugin via Bun
options.description: Use Tailwind's PostCSS integration instead of standalone CLI. Bun handles everything through its CSS pipeline. Tighter integration but PostCSS adds a layer.

build-pipeline.finding:
Build pipeline decision: Tailwind CLI v4 orchestrated by Bun scripts.

1. **Tailwind CSS v4 via @tailwindcss/cli**: Install `@tailwindcss/cli` and `daisyui` as Bun dependencies. Use Tailwind CLI as a standalone binary (NOT PostCSS plugin) — this is the recommended v4 approach and avoids PostCSS config complexity. DaisyUI v5 is imported via `@plugin "daisyui"` in the main CSS file.

2. **Bun orchestrates all build steps**: The existing Bun-based build system (build-css.ts, build-js.ts or equivalent) gains a Tailwind compilation step. Bun scripts invoke `bunx @tailwindcss/cli -i src/input.css -o dist/output.css` (or equivalent) alongside the existing TS→JS bundling. Both CSS and JS outputs go through content hashing and manifest.json generation.

3. **Unified dev/watch mode**: A single `bun run dev` command spawns both Tailwind CLI in watch mode (`--watch`) and the existing JS/TS watcher. Bun orchestrates both processes (e.g., via a dev.ts script using `Bun.spawn` for parallel processes).

4. **CSS file consolidation**: The current `build-css.ts` that concatenates multiple CSS files is replaced by a single Tailwind input file (e.g., `input.css`) that imports Tailwind base/components/utilities and the DaisyUI plugin. Custom theme overrides and any remaining custom CSS live in this file or are `@import`-ed. The old concatenation approach is eliminated.

5. **Manifest integration unchanged**: The existing content-hashing + manifest.json pattern stays the same — Tailwind's compiled CSS output simply replaces the old concatenated CSS as the input to the hashing/manifest step.

6. **Template scanning**: Tailwind v4's automatic content detection should work since it scans all files by default, but the Rust template strings (where classes are defined) may need explicit `@source` directives in the CSS file to ensure Tailwind finds classes in `.rs` files.

theme-strategy.scope: Theme migration: Mapping 3 custom dark themes (sneak/blue, arch/green, mech/orange) to DaisyUI v5 theme system. Visual direction and CSS override strategy.

questions.text:
What visual direction for the DaisyUI theme migration?

Current look: Pure black backgrounds, monospace font everywhere, terminal/hacker aesthetic with 3 color accents (blue, green, orange).

cons: More custom CSS overrides on top of DaisyUI
CRT effects can impact performance on low-end devices
Monospace everywhere limits readability for long text
options.description: Preserve the terminal vibe with DaisyUI's structure: zero border-radius, monospace everywhere, black backgrounds, CRT glow effects on text, scanline overlays, phosphor bloom on hover. Map primary color per theme (blue/green/orange). Compact TUI density like htop/lazygit.
options.label: CRT Terminal Aesthetic (Recommended)
pros: Keeps the unique identity of the project
DaisyUI handles component structure, custom CSS adds flavor
Terminal aesthetic is distinctive and fits P2P/CLI tool

cons: Looks like every other dark-mode app
options.description: Use DaisyUI's dark theme patterns with your 3 accent colors. Subtle rounded corners, system font stack for UI text, monospace only for code/filenames. Clean, professional look.
options.label: Modern Dark Theme
pros: Less custom CSS - more DaisyUI defaults

cons: Compromise - may not fully satisfy either direction
options.description: Keep monospace and black backgrounds but drop CRT effects. Small border-radius (2px), subtle borders instead of glow. Terminal-inspired but not retro. Clean readability with terminal personality.
options.label: Hybrid: Terminal Structure, Modern Polish

config.question:
What visual direction for the DaisyUI theme migration?

Current look: Pure black backgrounds, monospace font everywhere, terminal/hacker aesthetic with 3 color accents (blue, green, orange).

questions.text: How should the 3 custom themes (sneak/blue, arch/green, mech/orange) map to DaisyUI v5?

config.question: How should the 3 custom themes (sneak/blue, arch/green, mech/orange) map to DaisyUI v5?
config.context: [Theme migration: Mapping 3 custom dark themes (sneak/blue, arch/green, mech/orange) to DaisyUI v5 theme system. Visual direction and CSS override strategy.] You chose CRT terminal aesthetic. DaisyUI v5 themes are defined as CSS custom properties. We need to decide how to implement 3 dark themes with pure black backgrounds, monospace fonts, and CRT effects while leveraging DaisyUI's theme system.

options.label: Extend DaisyUI's 'black' theme 3 ways
options.description: Create 3 custom DaisyUI themes (sneak, arch, mech) that extend the built-in 'black' theme, overriding primary/secondary/accent colors per theme. Add a shared @layer for CRT effects (scanlines, glow, monospace). Theme switching via data-theme attribute.

options.label: Build 3 themes from scratch in DaisyUI format
options.description: Define all ~30 DaisyUI color tokens from scratch per theme for maximum control. More work but ensures no inherited styles leak through. Each theme gets exact hex values matching current design.

options.label: Minimal DaisyUI themes + CSS variable overrides
options.description: Use DaisyUI's 'dark' theme as a base, then override via a separate CSS layer that sets --color-primary etc. per theme. CRT effects in the same layer. Keeps DaisyUI config minimal but splits theme logic across files.

theme-strategy.finding:
Theme strategy decision: CRT/terminal aesthetic with 3 custom DaisyUI v5 themes extending the dark base.

1. **Visual direction — CRT Terminal**: Maintain the pure black backgrounds, monospace-everywhere, terminal/hacker aesthetic. DaisyUI components get styled to feel like CRT terminal UI elements — sharp corners (no border-radius or minimal), subtle glow effects on accent colors, scanline-optional overlays. The goal is DaisyUI's structure with a retro-terminal skin, not a generic modern UI.

2. **3 custom themes extending DaisyUI v5's dark base**: Each theme (sneak/blue, arch/green, mech/orange) is defined as a custom DaisyUI v5 theme in the Tailwind CSS file using `@plugin "daisyui"` with theme configuration. Each extends the `dark` base theme, overriding:
   - `primary` → the accent color (blue #hex, green #hex, orange #hex respectively)
   - `primary-content` → text on primary
   - `base-100` / `base-200` / `base-300` → pure black / near-black scale
   - `base-content` → light monospace text (white/light gray)
   - `secondary`, `accent`, `neutral` → mapped to complementary dark tones per theme
   - `info`, `success`, `warning`, `error` → consistent across all 3 themes (shared semantic colors)

3. **Theme switching mechanism**: Keep the existing theme-switching approach (likely a cookie or localStorage preference), but use DaisyUI's `data-theme` attribute on `<html>` to swap themes. The 3 themes are named `sneak`, `arch`, `mech` in the DaisyUI config.

4. **CRT effects via custom CSS layer**: Scanline overlays, text-shadow glow on accent colors, and any CRT-specific effects live in a small custom CSS layer (`@layer components { ... }`) in the Tailwind input file — NOT in DaisyUI theme config. This keeps theme definitions clean (just colors/typography) while the CRT aesthetic is applied universally via utility classes and component overrides.

5. **Typography**: `font-family: monospace` (or a specific mono font like JetBrains Mono / Fira Code) applied globally via Tailwind's `fontFamily` theme config. All DaisyUI components inherit this. No serif/sans-serif anywhere.

6. **Border radius override**: Global `--rounded-btn: 0; --rounded-box: 0;` (or DaisyUI v5 equivalent) to eliminate rounded corners across all components, reinforcing the terminal aesthetic.

migration-phasing.scope: Migration approach and phasing: Big-bang vs incremental, phase ordering, keeping the app functional during migration.

questions.text:
What migration strategy should we use?

Key constraint: Templates are Rust strings with inline hx-* attributes. Running HTMX and Datastar simultaneously on the same page is problematic.

cons: Phase 1 touches every template for CSS classes
Phase 2 still a big-bang for the interaction layer
options.description: Phase 1: Build pipeline + Tailwind/DaisyUI (HTMX stays working). Phase 2: HTMX→Datastar swap (big-bang for interaction layer, CSS already stable). Phase 3: Cleanup and polish.
options.label: CSS-First Coexistence (Recommended)
pros: Visual changes visible immediately for feedback
Interaction swap is isolated to Phase 2

cons: Harder to debug - everything changes at once
options.description: Rewrite everything at once: new build pipeline, new CSS, new templates with Datastar attributes, new TS entry point. One PR, one switch.
options.label: Full Big-Bang Rewrite

options.description: Migrate one page at a time: files page first, then peers, settings, editor. Each page gets both CSS and Datastar treatment.
options.label: Page-by-Page Migration

config.question:
What migration strategy should we use?

Key constraint: Templates are Rust strings with inline hx-* attributes. Running HTMX and Datastar simultaneously on the same page is problematic.

questions.text:
How should the CSS-first migration phases be structured?

You chose CSS-first: migrate DaisyUI/Tailwind styling before touching HTMX→Datastar. I'm recommending a 3-phase approach where Phase 1 replaces all custom CSS with DaisyUI classes while HTMX still works, Phase 2 swaps HTMX→Datastar route-by-route, Phase 3 cleans up.

config.question:
How should the CSS-first migration phases be structured?

You chose CSS-first: migrate DaisyUI/Tailwind styling before touching HTMX→Datastar. I'm recommending a 3-phase approach where Phase 1 replaces all custom CSS with DaisyUI classes while HTMX still works, Phase 2 swaps HTMX→Datastar route-by-route, Phase 3 cleans up.

options.label: 3 strict phases (CSS → Datastar → Cleanup)
options.description: Phase 1: Replace all custom CSS with Tailwind/DaisyUI classes across every template, keeping HTMX intact. Phase 2: Convert all routes from HTMX partials to Datastar SSE, one route group at a time. Phase 3: Remove old CSS, dead code, finalize. No phase overlap.

options.label: 3 phases with overlap allowed
options.description: Same 3 phases but allow starting Datastar conversion on already-styled pages before ALL CSS migration is complete. More parallel work, slightly more risk of rework.

options.label: 4 granular phases (Build → CSS → Datastar → Cleanup)
options.description: Phase 0: Set up Tailwind/DaisyUI build pipeline with no template changes. Phase 1: CSS migration. Phase 2: Datastar conversion. Phase 3: Cleanup. Separates infrastructure from styling work.

config.context: [Migration approach and phasing: Big-bang vs incremental, phase ordering, keeping the app functional during migration.] I'm recommending 4 phases because separating build pipeline setup (Phase 0) from actual CSS migration means you can merge the infrastructure change independently and catch build issues early. This aligns with the build-pipeline branch choosing Tailwind CLI via Bun.

migration-phasing.finding:
Migration phasing decision: CSS-first, 4-phase granular approach.

**Phase 1 — Build Pipeline Setup**: Add Tailwind CSS v4 + DaisyUI v5 to the Bun build system. Get the toolchain working with a single input.css file, @plugin "daisyui" import, and 3 custom themes (sneak/blue, arch/green, mech/orange) defined as DaisyUI theme extensions. Tailwind output replaces the old concatenated CSS in the manifest pipeline. All existing HTMX functionality continues working — zero behavioral changes. Deliverable: `bun run dev` builds both Tailwind CSS and JS, old CSS build step removed.

**Phase 2 — CSS/Component Migration**: Replace all custom CSS classes with DaisyUI component classes + Tailwind utilities in the Rust template strings. Work page-by-page (login, dashboard, settings, etc.). HTMX attributes (hx-get, hx-post, hx-swap, hx-target) remain untouched — only class names and HTML structure change. Theme switcher updated to use DaisyUI's data-theme attribute. CRT/terminal aesthetic applied via theme overrides. Deliverable: App looks fully DaisyUI-styled, still powered by HTMX.

**Phase 3 — HTMX→Datastar Conversion**: Build the SSE builder abstraction layer. Convert routes one-by-one: replace hx-* attributes with data-on-*/data-* Datastar attributes in templates, convert corresponding Axum handlers from returning HTML partials to returning SSE merge-fragments responses. Remove HX-Request header checks. Each route is a self-contained unit of work. WebSocket handlers remain unchanged. Deliverable: All routes use Datastar SSE, HTMX dependency removed.

**Phase 4 — Cleanup & Polish**: Remove HTMX JS from the bundle. Remove any legacy CSS files, dead template code, or compatibility shims. Optimize SSE response patterns. Final cross-theme visual QA across all 3 themes. Performance audit on SSE streaming. Deliverable: Clean codebase, no HTMX remnants.

**Key principle**: The app remains fully functional at the end of every phase. Phase boundaries are natural stopping points where the team can ship, test, and course-correct. Phases 1-2 (CSS migration) are lower-risk and can proceed quickly. Phase 3 (Datastar conversion) is the highest-risk phase and benefits from the route-by-route granularity — if issues arise, only one route is affected at a time.
